nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated rocky hill streams and torrential upland rivers; typically found clinging to boulder surfaces and smooth rock faces in areas of strong current

decorPreferences: smooth water-worn boulders and flat rocks placed in direct flow path
cobbles and gravel substrate for grazing surface area
established biofilm and algae growth on rock surfaces
anubias or java fern attached to rocks (not naturally encountered but tolerated)
tight-fitting cover — will climb glass and escape

diet.notes: Aufwuchs grazer in the wild — continuously browses biofilm, benthic algae, diatoms, and microorganisms from rock surfaces. In captivity supplement with algae wafers, spirulina-based sinking pellets, blanched spinach or zucchini, and Repashy gel foods. Frozen bloodworm and daphnia accepted sparingly; excessive protein intake has been linked to internal health issues in this genus. A mature tank with well-established algae growth on rocks is strongly recommended for long-term success.

avoidWith: Butterfly Hillstream Loach (Beaufortia kweichowensis) — larger, dominant competitor for grazing territory

summary: A small (~5 cm) Bornean hillstream loach restricted to the Sadong basin of Sarawak. Demands cool 20–24°C water, very high flow, and saturated dissolved oxygen — a dedicated river-biotope setup is essential. Constant aufwuchs grazer; distinguished from the Butterfly Hillstream Loach (Beaufortia kweichowensis) by its smaller Gastromyzon genus and spotted patterning.
careNotes:
Gastromyzon scitulus is one of approximately 36 Gastromyzon species endemic to Borneo, all restricted to fast-flowing, oxygen-rich hill streams. In the wild it inhabits tributaries of the Sungai Sadong basin in southern Sarawak, Malaysia, clinging to boulder surfaces in strong current. Replicating these conditions in the aquarium is the central challenge of keeping this species.

Flow and oxygenation are non-negotiable: aim for a turnover of 10–15× the tank volume per hour using an oversized canister filter plus one or more powerheads or spray bars directed across the bottom. The water must be highly oxygenated at all times — even brief lapses in flow have caused losses. Temperature must be kept cool (20–24°C); warmer values depress dissolved oxygen and stress fish accustomed to cold upland streams. A chiller or cold basement placement is often necessary in warm climates.

Water quality must be excellent. Gastromyzon are sensitive to nitrate accumulation; keep nitrates below 20 ppm with regular water changes. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12) replicates native stream chemistry.

Tank décor should maximise the total surface area of smooth, flat rocks placed directly in the current path — these become the primary feeding and resting surfaces. Bright lighting accelerates algae and biofilm growth, which underpins the species' nutrition. A minimum group of four individuals is recommended; conspecific territorial skirmishes occur among males but rarely cause injury in adequately sized tanks.

Note on similar species: G. scitulus is often confused with the Butterfly Hillstream Loach (Beaufortia kweichowensis, fw-fish-158), which belongs to a different genus, grows noticeably larger, and has a broader, more wing-like pectoral fin profile. G. scitulus is distinguished by its smaller adult size (~5 cm), characteristic cream spots on a dark body, and iridescent blue caudal fin streaks in life.

A tight-fitting cover is essential — hillstream loaches readily climb glass and will escape through any gap.
breedingNotes: Breeding G. scitulus in captivity is rarely achieved and remains poorly documented. One reported method involves maintaining adults in a well-established river biotope tank for several months, then conditioning over 6–8 weeks with gradual temperature elevation, followed by a return to cooler temperatures (20–21°C) combined with increased water changes to simulate seasonal conditions. Females are noticeably plumper than males; mature males develop raised tubercle-like ridges on the leading pectoral fin rays. When spawning occurs, females scatter non-adhesive eggs over rocky substrate; eggs reportedly hatch within approximately 3 days. Fry are extremely small and require infusoria-type foods and established biofilm surfaces. Commercial breeding is essentially unknown; virtually all specimens in the hobby are wild-caught from Sarawak.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Gastromyzon scitulus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (40–45 mm SL), temperature (20–24°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (18–215 ppm ≈ 1–12 dGH), native range (Sungai Sadong basin, southern Sarawak), tank dimensions (75×30 cm base), diet (benthic algae and biofilm, gelatin foods), breeding account (temperature conditioning, 100+ eggs, ~3-day hatch), and group-keeping recommendation (4+).

additional.name: FishBase — Gastromyzon scitulus (ID 63180)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, Cypriniformes), native distribution (Serian, Sadong basin, Sarawak, Malaysia), habitat (fast flowing rocky streams), maximum size (31.3 mm SL recorded), trophic level (2.2, consistent with herbivore/aufwuchs), and IUCN Vulnerable status (assessed 2019).

additional.name: Loaches Online — Gastromyzon scitulus
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (~5 cm / 2 inches), temperature (68–75°F / 20–23.8°C), pH (7.0–8.0), hardness (max 12 dH), habitat (fast-flowing streams over boulders, very high oxygen requirements), diet (flake, sinking pellets, algae wafers, bloodworm, blanched vegetables), sexual dimorphism (females plumper; males with raised pectoral fin ray ridges), and that breeding in aquaria has not been reliably achieved.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder — Borneo Sucker Detailed Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary Gastromyzon genus care data: temperature (18–25°C), pH (6.5–8.0), gH (2–15 dGH), lifespan (3–4 years), minimum tank 75 L for group, flow rate (10–15× per hour), compatible tankmates (rasboras, Otocinclus, Pygmy Corydoras, filter shrimp), and protein-restriction dietary note.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Care Guide for Hillstream Loaches
additional.notes: General hillstream loach husbandry (article focuses on Sewellia lineolata but principles apply to Gastromyzon): temperature range 20–27°C, tight-fitting hood requirement, Repashy gel food and sinking wafers recommended, pre-filter sponge for fry protection, peaceful community compatibility.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding is rarely if ever achieved in home aquaria; virtually all trade specimens are wild-caught from Sarawak. Conditioning attempts involve prolonged temperature cycling and matured river-biotope tanks. Females scatter non-adhesive eggs over rocks; fry require infusoria-type foods.
